text: Single-Sn-atom catalysts with tunable coordination environment exhibit outstanding performance in selective electrochemical CO2 reduction to formic acid. Herein, a series of CO2RR on Sn single-atom sites is explored by designing a series of single-Sn-atom catalysts with well-defined structures. It is demonstrated that Sn(IV)-N4 moieties axially coordinated with oxygen species (O-Sn-N4) on Sn single-atom sites give rise to selectivity and activity for formic acid production, reaching a maximum Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 89.4% with a partial current density (jHCOOH) of 74.8 mA cm-2 at -1.0 V vs RHE. Through a multi-technique operando approach, combining X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS), Raman spectroscopy, and 119Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy, the existence of surface-bound bidentate tin carbonate species is identified during CO2RR. In addition, the electronic and coordination states of single-Sn-atom species during CO2RR are characterized. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that Sn-O-CO2 species preferentially form over O-Sn-N4 sites, which modulates the adsorption configurations of reactive intermediates and reduces the energy barrier for hydrogenation of *OCHO species in comparison with that of forming *COOH species over Sn-N4 sites, thus enhancing the conversion of CO2 to HCOOH.
